question_text: In 2009, what is the quantity of tea that can be bought for ₹2,000?
explanation:
Line charts are typically used for trend analysis for chronological data. A clear trend of price change can be seen through the line chart—with a word of caution because gap in number of years between two data point is not same. Table given below presents the index value for Beverages, based upon the data from line chart, and two other items. As stated in the first line, you may expect questions which ask to compare the trends of different items across the years. Solution and Explanation for 4 to 7

This question needs a simple calculation. Price per kg of tea in 2009 = ₹170/kg. Hence quantity that can be bought for 1000 ₹1000 = = ₹5.88/kg 170 Hence option (b) is the answer.

directions: Directions for questions 1 to 3: Go through the infor-
question_text: During which period was the change in the price (₹/kg/years) the lowest for coffee quality A?
explanation:
Line charts are typically used for trend analysis for chronological data. A clear trend of price change can be seen through the line chart—with a word of caution because gap in number of years between two data point is not same. Table given below presents the index value for Beverages, based upon the data from line chart, and two other items. As stated in the first line, you may expect questions which ask to compare the trends of different items across the years. Solution and Explanation for 4 to 7

Question is asking for change, and not increase or decrease. So we are required to work upon with magnitude only, and sign (positive or negative) is not important. Year Span Total Total Change/ Change Years year 2000 – 2005 75 5 15 2005 – 2007 105 2 52.5 2007 – 2008 80 1 80 2008 – 2009 65 1 65 It is obvious that change/year is minimum in the year 2000 – 2005. Hence option (a) is the answer.

Line charts are typically used for trend analysis for chronological data. Index has the following pattern: Increase – Increase – Decrease – Decrease Only Coffee Quality A shows this trend. Hence option (d) is the answer.

directions: Directions for questions 1 to 3: Go through the infor-
question_text: Assuming that equal weightage is given to each of three beverages in calculating the index, which one contributes most to the rise in index from 2000 to 2009?
explanation:
Line charts are typically used for trend analysis for chronological data. A clear trend of price change can be seen through the line chart—with a word of caution because gap in number of years between two data point is not same. Table given below presents the index value for Beverages, based upon the data from line chart, and two other items. As stated in the first line, you may expect questions which ask to compare the trends of different items across the years. Solution and Explanation for 4 to 7

Beverages Index in the year 2000 = 100 Beverages Index in the year 2009 = 104. So there is a rise in index. It can be observed that Tea price has remained same and price of Coffee Quality A has gone down in 2009 over 2000. Tea Price - Gone DOWN Beverages Index - Gone UP Coﬀee Coﬀee Quality B Quality A price - price - Constant Gone UP Only the price of Coffee Quality A has gone up. So it can be concluded that Coffee quality A has the maximum impact on the beverages index and hence contributes most. Hence option (c) is the answer.

directions: Directions for questions 8 to 11: Go through the chart
question_text: If the total work force was 76 million whereas the total value added was ₹225 million, then which of the following had the maximum value addition per worker?
explanation:
Table provides percentage share (or composition) of Factories, Employment, Fixed Capital, Variable cost and Value added among four sectors viz. Government sector, Public sector, Private sector and Joint sector. Key point here is to understand that Government sector is broken down into three sub sectors. For all the data given in Government sector, corresponding data for sub sectors is provided in the bar chart. Solution and Explanations for 8 to 11 Value Added

Value addition per worker = No. of Employes Total value added = ₹225 million and Total work force = 76 million. We go by the options and calculate Value added per worker for all the given options: 10% of ₹ 225 million For Central = 6% of 76 million 3% of ₹ 225 million For State = 6% of 76 million 12% of ₹ 225 million For Central/State = 3% of 76 million 8% of ₹ 225 million For Public = 8% of 76 million Calculation Short Cut: Inserted of calculating the real value in the above four options, what we can do is quick ratio ₹ 225 million comparison. We can observe that 76 million is present in all the ratios. Let us assume ₹ 225 million = N. 76 million So the four ratios become like the following: 10% of 225 million 10% For Central = = 6% of 76 million 6% 3% of ₹ 225 million 3% For State = = N 6% of 76 million 6% 12% of ₹ 225 million For Central/State = 3% of 76 million 12% = N 3% 8% of ₹ 225 million 8% For Public = = N 8% of 76 million 8% It can be now clearly seen that the ratio is largest in case of Central/State. Hence option (c) is the answer. In such questions, we can do the proceeding in a nutshell like the following: Value added per worker for various sectors will be proportional to following ratios: Central 10/6 State 3/6 Central/state 12/3 Public 8/8

question_text: Which of the following sectors has the maximum fixed capital invested per factory?
explanation:
Table provides percentage share (or composition) of Factories, Employment, Fixed Capital, Variable cost and Value added among four sectors viz. Government sector, Public sector, Private sector and Joint sector. Key point here is to understand that Government sector is broken down into three sub sectors. For all the data given in Government sector, corresponding data for sub sectors is provided in the bar chart. Solution and Explanations for 8 to 11 Value Added

Using the method given in A1: Fixed capital per factory will be proportional to the following ratios: Central 5/8 State 4/4 Central/state 5/6 Public 6/12 Hence option (b) is the answer.

directions: Directions for questions 8 to 11: Go through the chart
question_text: If the variable is proportional to the number of employees and the production per employee, then for which of the following is the production highest?
explanation:
Table provides percentage share (or composition) of Factories, Employment, Fixed Capital, Variable cost and Value added among four sectors viz. Government sector, Public sector, Private sector and Joint sector. Key point here is to understand that Government sector is broken down into three sub sectors. For all the data given in Government sector, corresponding data for sub sectors is provided in the bar chart. Solution and Explanations for 8 to 11 Value Added

If the variables are proportional to the number of employees and the production per employee, then we can say that the variable cost is directly proportional to the production. Since the variable cost is maximum for private sector, it will also have the maximum production. Hence option (b) is the answer.
